sentences.text: Yeah, well we weren't yet together, but I'll make it quick because, you know, it's-- what happened was that, after a while, I was put in the same hospital as my sister, and after about a month.
Then the nuns said I-- you know, said I should go to synagogue, I should pray.
You know, they didn't try to convert me or anything like that.
So I remember them taking me to synagogue once in Maastricht and [? during ?] I was-- I got-- I didn't feel well, so they took me back.
But anyway, after two months in the hospital, we were ready to leave-- but where to?
They didn't know where to send us, because we were in the way often.
They didn't know.
You know.
I didn't know.
We didn't know whether anybody was left alive.
We knew our mother had gone to England, but we hadn't heard anything from her after all those years, so we didn't know.
We assumed she was OK.
So first-- first thing they wanted to do--
The first thing they wanted to do was to register us, you know, like returning citizens, you know.

So that's when we registered with the Dutch authorities, or whatever they were at the time, and they-- the guy registering us was one of those real civil servants, and they said, wait a minute-- you were born Dinslaken, Germany.
You came to Holland 1938.
You're not Dutch.
You're DP, you know, displaced person.
I have to send you back to Germany.
So of course, we said no way.
And of course the nuns also said, no way.
They wouldn't-- they would never-- see, they said, even if it means we have to take a hospital bed, and we shouldn't, we're not going to allow them to-- we're not gonna let them free.
We're not-- not releasing them from the hospital if they have to be sent back to Germany.

Then, they came to an agreement with these-- with this people and-- and-- who registered us to take us out of the hospital and put us for a few days in-- in a school where some other Dutch non-Jewish children who were kept there for some reason or other, but we were free to go out whenever we wanted to.
We went, you know, restricted.
So we spent most of our day visiting the various-- they were very friendly, those nuns, so we went to visit them.
They had nothing really to do.
And then, they were just sort of trying to find a solution for us, the nuns, that [INAUDIBLE] in the hospital.
They heard that this same gentleman who had taken care was arrested, that Berenbaum was coming.
They didn't know that they had been with him, of course, but they heard that somebody had come back who had been on a different train with us-- than us.
You know, and also been taken out of-- he had been in Bergen-Belsen, and he had been on a different train, liberated by the Russians.
And two months, you know, after liberation, they came finally back to Holland.
This was June-- was July, I think, yeah, July in 1945.
And he was going through Maastricht on his way to Amsterdam, and he had some orphans with us.
So then the-- the nuns asked, would you mind going with them?
That's a good-- yes, of course not, we know him, you know.
Oh, that's good.
We were reunited with them and with their children.
You know, with their-- their children.
They had some other orphans with them, but we really didn't-- these were not people that had been with us in Westerbork.
We didn't know them, you know.
We may have known one or two of them from-- from Bergen-Belsen, but I don't really remember whether we knew them.
They took us on-- on trucks.
We were taken by Americans to Amsterdam.
And then from Amsterdam, we were taken to a little town called Laren, where there was a Jewish orphanage of sort of kids who had survived, or in hiding, or whatever.
And this is probably where he went with his children for the time being.
And in the meanwhile, this uncle, Adolf, who had disappeared right at the very beginning, had been in hiding all through the war.
He had managed to elude the Germans, and he was looking for us because he had seen on a Red Cross list that we had survived.
And he didn't know where we were, so then somebody told him, maybe they're in Maastricht.

So he tried to get to Maastricht, but in-- to get from Utrecht, where he lived, to Maastricht-- today is maybe an hour and a half by car, if that-- took him five days to get there because of the-- you know, everything was-- everything-- the roads were-- were bombed, had been bombed, and there was heavy-- had been heavy fighting there.
And by the time he got there, it was at the time [? already ?] the Jews had left.
So he made his way back, and then he found out we had gone to Laren.
About three or four days after we'd been in Laren, we were called to the administrative office by a Mr.
Berenbaum.
He was sitting there, and I walk in there.
And there is my uncle.
You know, which was a surprise, 'cause I didn't expect it.
You know.
He was-- was very emotional, you know, about it.
He cried.

And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] Anyway, he told us that he was living in Utrecht with my aunt and my cousin, you know, and that he already told our mother that he knew-- he knew we had survived-- he just didn't know where we were.
We hadn't been in touch with her, hadn't written to her.

And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- Anyway, we left-- we left the orphanage, and we went to Utrecht, you know, very exciting, because-- because I knew that the other-- the younger cousin had died-- that I knew.
We found out that the older cousin, you know, had actually, matter of fact, worked for the underground in the last year, even though she was in hiding.
She had very good papers, had been caught.
And, you know, in February of '45, but after being beaten up, she had been released because they couldn't really prove anything.
And then my uncle tried to make the arrangements for us to get to England to-- to see our-- get to our mother.
It wasn't very simple because the British were not anxious to receive anybody at that time, you know.
It took till about November of '45.
We stayed in Utrecht.
I went to school there, went back to school, to the Dutch school.
And then in November '45, finally we were given the OK.
We would go-- we'd go to England.
And so we went on an overnight ship from Hoek van Holland-- the same Hoek van Holland-- to [?
Harritz. ?] Then we went-- went by train to London, and there my mother was waiting for us.
You know, and-- it was very strange for me, actually, because I-- I really sort of was out of touch with my mother, if you know what I mean.
My sister was very important to her.
For me, too, but less.
You know, I could've-- if they would have said I can stay with my uncle, I would have stayed with my uncle.
You know.
This, you know, this is how it was.
But still, very quickly I-- we went to Leeds.
She was living in Leeds.
She was working still as a maid, but as a housekeeper, not as a maid.
She ran the household of a-- of a very wealthy Jewish industrialist who was a widow.
And she ran this.
You know, she mostly had the run of the house.
And they always had said to her, because she had gotten the-- started to work for them at-- in 1940, '41, that if your children survive, they can come and live here.
So we lived there.
And I went to learn English.
I went to school, and then in England there was a problem that if you didn't take certain exams at age 11, you couldn't go to-- to high school-- so which sort of put a stop to that for me.
But then, I became interested anyway in the Zionist Youth Movement.
OK.
I had seen in-- in the news and read the papers how the British were stopping the DPs from, you know, getting to Israel, what they called illegal immigration.
They called in all the ships with people from the camps-- you know about it, I'm sure.

And I sort of felt, you know, could have been me, just as easy, because if I wouldn't have been lucky and-- and have an uncle in Holland and a mother in England, I probably would have been put in DP camp, like-- and I would have been trying to get to Palestine.
So I joined a-- I first joined a religious movement-- well, my mother wanted to-- but I didn't find it suiting my purposes, because they were not that interested in-- in sending people to Israel.
So then I joined sort of a more left-wing socialist movement called Hashomer Hatzair.
And their purpose was, actually, to educate the young to go to Israel at a certain age.
And I stayed with them, and after a number of years in Leeds, I went on [?
Achshara, ?] which was to prepare for going on-- on to Israel.
Because at the time already was Israel, by the time I went on [?
Achshara. ?]
And then in 1952, finally I was-- went to Israel.
I was in a kibbutz in Israel.
I was in Israel for six years, till '58.
I served in the army, also-- like everybody.
And then in '58, my mother and my sister meanwhile immigrated to Chile from England, because there was nobody in England.
And, you know, the sisters of my brother-- of my father who had immigrated just before the war to Chile, they were all living there.
And Uncle Adolf also had immigrated to Chile, also decided-- he left Holland and he went to-- to Chile.
So I wanted to visit.
So I visited-- went to visit Chile, and I stayed there for five years.
It was a long visit.
You know.
I learned Spanish.
And I-- I worked a little bit, and then I decided Chile really wasn't what I wanted.
You know, even though my mother would have liked me to stay, and my sister, it really wasn't my-- where I wanted to be.
So it was either go back to Israel, or maybe I was going to try to live to see what it was like here.
I was sort of adventurous.
I was single.
So I came to the United States.
I had an uncle in Chicago.
Sort of-- you know, you had-- in order to get a visa, had to have an affidavit in-- in those days, but it wasn't difficult to get a new visa because there was-- I was under the German quota.
There was a quota system still then.
The German quota wasn't filled because of the prosperity at that time.
In Germany, nobody-- nobody immigrated from there, so I had no problem getting a visa and I came here.
And-- When?
When?
I came here in 1963.
I-- I-- I left Israel in 1958.
I was-- from 19-- from the end of '58 till November '63, I was in Chile.
Then I came here.
And I came here actually not in November-- I came here in October, October '63.
And I got a job right away working in the Italian-- not-- yeah, not in Italian, in the ZIM lines-- I wanted to work in the Italian line because I worked in Chile in the Italian line.
But they didn't really want me, so I went to ZIM lines.
I spoke Hebrew, so they-- and they were just getting a new ship, so they hired me there.
Why do I remember it was October?
Because in November, it was when Kennedy was assassinated.
I was just here one month.
That's sort of-- I remember that very well.
I was in shock.
And then I worked there for five years, and then ZIM lines, they went out of the passenger business.
So I went to look for another job, and I worked-- started to work in El Al through recommendations of a friend.
I worked for [?
Al, ?] and that's how I met my wife.
I went-- was sent on a course to Israel-- in-- when was it?
Yeah, I was sent on a course to Israel.
And I have an aunt in Israel who assists [? of ?] my mother, and I went to visit her at the end of the course.
And-- and my wife's parents were-- I had known my aunt for many years, since before the war.
They had been together even, for a while.
And my aunt lived in a town called [?
Qasaba, ?] and they lived in a town called Yokne'am.
That's where my wife was born.
When they went to visit my wife's sister, who lived in Ashkelon, they always went by [?
Qasaba. ?] They used to go and visit.
So I-- I was there when-- when they came and visited, and I met-- that's where I met my wife.
And we sort of corresponded, and then I went to see her.
And we decided to get married.
This was in-- in 1969 when we got married, OK.
And I brought her here.
And we lived first in Ocean Township, and then we moved.
We built this house, and then we moved here in 1970.
We have three children, the oldest one, Omri, he's just finishing Police Academy.
He's gonna be a policeman.
He wants to be a policeman.
He finished college, and now he's in Police Academy.
The middle one, Yuval, is autistic.
We don't know yet what we're gonna do with him.
He's going to be 21 this March.
My oldest one, by the way, is 23.
He's gonna be 21, and when 21-- when they reach age 21, the-- the state no longer has any obligation to teach them.
You know, up to then, the local school system had to take care of him, so they used to-- they transport him to-- to a special school.
So we're now fighting with the state to get an adult-- to get him into an adult program and get him into a group home.
And then my youngest daughter, Avital, she's 16.
She goes to high school here in Howell.
So that brings me up to the present day.
Would you like to see my wife and daughter now?
This is a pick-up-- Mr.
Spiegel and his family.
OK.
I-- this is only part of my family.
I have three children.
My-- this is my wife, Yael. 20-- we are married for 26 years.
This is my daughter, Avital.
She's 16.
Like I said, I have an-- my oldest is a son, Omri.
He's 23, and Yuval is 21.
But he's gonna be 21 in March.
He's 20, all right.
She's gonna be 17 in a bit.
All right, right.
You're right.
Anyway, like I said, we're married for 26 years.
And we live in this house for about 24 years.
Yeah.
Right?
No.
[LAUGHS]
23 years, whatever.
Yeah, yeah.
We'll be here 23 this February.
23 years, yeah, 23 years, OK.
Not quite.
It doesn't make that much difference.
I work for [?
El Al ?] still, like I said before.

And I've never-- my wife didn't-- knew that I was in a camp-- I think she knew when we got married that I was in camps-- but she never really-- I never talk much about it, you know, because I find it very difficult.
And I wanted to forget.
I mean, I think like-- like everybody else, I think it's not a-- not a part of your life you-- you think back upon as-- as being good, so you try to forget about it and get on with your life.

And one of reason-- one of the reasons I started to-- to talk about it-- one of the reasons was because I saw so many cases where there were deniers, what are called Holocaust deniers, and they were getting a lot of publicity.
And I felt that if we don't speak, those of us who survived, we give them more ammunition.
And very soon-- I mean, I'm a child survivor.
You know, I was 13 when I was liberated.
Very soon there's going to be nobody left to-- to speak about it.
So the first thing we did is six-- wow, it's almost seven-- six-and-a-half years ago, I took my family-- that's except for Yuval, for the middle one-- Omri, Avital, and Yael on a trip to Europe.
Europe was Holland and Germany.
We went-- we saw-- we saw some-- we went-- went to Westerbork and to Bergen-Belsen, even though there's nothing to see in Bergen-Belsen.
Nothing to see in Westerbork at the time, either-- very-- very little.
That's not true.
There was very little to see.
I mean, in Bergen-Belsen, all they had was the mass graves.
You know, Bergen-Belsen was burned to the ground by the British because of the disease.
And you see huge mass graves here.
There was a [?
Muslim ?] museum.
2,500-- 2,500 bodies here or 5,000 dead.
There's a museum, sure, but the camp itself-- you can only-- only imagine.
You can't really see it anymore.
Some other camps are still there, but this one isn't.
And at the time in Westerbork, now they've changed it.
There was a memorial there, too.
You know.

There-- first there was a museum, a small museum, and then you walk for about three kilometers, you come to the camp, and at the very end they had a sort of memorial with a watch tower and like a railroad which-- to [?
Norway. ?] Like, the-- the rails were pointed to heaven.
This was the introduction into-- to what, you know, happened to me.
And the trip wasn't only that, obviously.
You can't take kids only on that.
But this is when I started to-- to get involved.
That's when you started getting involved.
You started talking before.
I think they--
Yeah, but--
One-- one-- a few things that really helped was, first of all, your first heart attack, I think, nine-- almost nine years ago.
Yeah, yeah.
I guess start opening up, and I guess.
Yeah, but--
Simply the years-- the amount of years-- so many years had to go by.
But really to speak to schools and things like that, I only started after--
That's later.
I started after that.
But to me you start-- in trickles, I heard over the years, I heard bits and pieces.
Also, you know, when you see what goes on in the world today, you say to yourself, well, maybe not exactly the same thing can happen again.
But it-- but it can happen again.
You know.
It can even happen to the Jews, you know, who knows.
You know, this-- this-- like a famous professor said, you know, this wasn't just evil people who did this.
These were ordinary people who did this, ordinary like-- like everybody else.
You know.
It wasn't any special people, you know, who became-- who did these evil things.
And-- and-- people are very easily-led, and-- under certain conditions, especially.

You know, if you get a lot of unemployed, or you get a strong leader, you know, a strong person who-- who easily convinces people-- if you look what happens in Yugoslavia, if you look what happens in-- in-- in other places in the world, in Africa especially-- it's-- it's very-- what's the matter?
I have to go.
Oh, OK.
It's very easy to-- that things can happen again.
And-- and-- so, you know, I really feel that it's very important for the children to learn what happened to us, because the Holocaust was a unique experience.
This was an attempt to wipe out a race, the Jews.
You know, other-- other so-called holocausts were really for land.
People wanted to-- to push the-- the Armenians out of certain areas when the Armenians in Armenia at one time were persecuted.
But Armenians living in-- in Istanbul were not touched because they were no threat.
You know, they were not-- but the Jews were-- were-- were to be exterminated wherever they were.
You know, they were the enemy.
You're becoming a big historian.
Yeah.
And-- and so it's really something which the kids-- if-- if the children don't learn about it, then it'll be lost in history.
And I always say, when-- once we forget our past, it can happen again in the future.
You know, this is-- this is my message, you know, that we should remember where we came from, should remember our past, what happened to us.
As long as we remember, we have to forgive but we cannot forget.
Once we forget, then we have the danger of it happening again.

And this is why it's so important to teach the young, because they are the ones who-- the-- the next generation are the ones who are going to continue and-- and talk about it-- what they heard from their parents to the generations after.
OK.
This is the Iron Cross.
My father got the-- this decoration in the First World War.
This is one of many, what he got.
This particular one was given to him in 1914.
This is-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK.
This is my birth certificate, which was issued in 1938.
All the birth certificates up to then were-- were taken away, and these were re-issued.
And if you notice, it says on it 'Fritz Israel Spiegel.' My name is not Israel, but every Jewish male had to be Israel, and every female, Sarah.
This is-- this is a copy-- this is one of these-- not a copy-- this is original of a Red Cross letter, which we sent to my mother.
She sent to us, and we replied on it.
You could send a letter through the Red Cross every month and write 25 words.
So you said, we're feeling fine.
Everything is good, and-- and-- hope to hear from you again soon.
This is a DP card which was filled out in Maastricht by the authorities, affirm that we were repatriated.
And-- and this particular card-- as we were not Dutch citizens, we were given a DP card because we were foreigners, and they considered us displaced persons.
This is a picture of the local soccer team in Dinslaken.
Of my father was part of the soccer team.
This is in 1913, '14, before the war, just before the war--
First World War.
He is the third from the right.
Yeah.
This is my father and my mother.
I think it's a photo taken, oh, at their wedding or at the honeymoon.
This is my sister as a baby.
This was a very popular way of-- everybody, I think, has a picture like that in Germany, when they were babies.
And this is my father and mother, and my sister and myself, when I was a little baby.
This is a picture taken in Hamborn, where I stand in front of the flowers.
And that's my mother behind there with two aunts and-- and a friend of my mother's.
One-- one is Aunt Anna, who lives in Israel.
The other one is my Aunt-- Aunt Marthe, who was married to my uncle Adolf.
This is the wedding picture of my aunt [?
Greta, ?] or Margareta, which was her name, really when she-- she married a-- a Dutch gentleman called Hertog Weijel, and they lived in Enschede, Holland.
Unfortunately, they were killed during the war.
The whole family was.
This is the house where we lived in Holland, in Dinxperlo.
This is the house of Uncle Max.
It's in [?
Polstraat ?] in Dinxperlo.
This is in 1946, in England.
My mother and my sister and myself after the war in 1946.
This is a picture of my mother with my aunt, who now lives in Argentina, when the-- when the aunt went to visit her in-- in-- in Chile.
It was taken a few years ago.
Aunt Marthe.
Marthe.
My mother had three sisters.
There are four together.
This is Marthe.
There was Anna, who lives in Israel, and Margareta, [?
Greta, ?] who got killed in the war.
The only one I would have liked was-- you had the original of this, don't you want to use that?
OK, this is a picture taken some time in-- in the summer of '42.
This is my maternal grandfather, Julius Berghausen, myself, my sister, and Sophie Beckhousen-- who actually is not my grandmother.
It-- it's my grandfather's second wife.
My grandmother died in 1919.
OK.
I think that's enough on that.
